F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: On a first home visit or a special occasion invitation, the guest brings a small gift to show appreciation for hosting.

GPT-5.2.name: Emina Bešić
GPT-5.2.story:
Emina checked the address again as she walked through a residential street in Sarajevo, heading to her coworker Selim’s apartment for the first time. In her tote bag she carried a small box of chocolates and a packet of ground coffee wrapped in brown paper.

At the door she rang and adjusted the bag handles. Selim opened with a smile. “Emina, welcome!”

Emina stepped forward and offered the bag immediately. “Thank you for inviting me. I brought a little something,” she said.

Selim took it with both hands. “You did not have to.”

“I know, but here it is,” Emina replied, slipping her shoes off at the entry and placing them neatly beside a row of sneakers.

Selim’s wife, Maja, came from the kitchen. Emina greeted her with a handshake and a quick cheek kiss. “Nice to meet you, Maja.”

Maja glanced at the bag. “Oh, coffee—super.”

Emina followed them into the living room, keeping her coat on until Selim pointed to a hanger. “You can put it there,” he said.

As Maja went to put the chocolates on the table, Emina added, “Just tell me where to sit,” and perched on the edge of the sofa.

Selim brought small cups and said, “Coffee?” Emina nodded. “That works, thank you,” and reached for the cup with both hands.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the guest had no time to shop, the guest brings a simple token like coffee and expresses sincere thanks for the invitation.

GPT-5.2.name: Alen Čengić
GPT-5.2.story:
Alen got off work late in Sarajevo and realized the small shop near his building would close soon. He stopped at a kiosk and grabbed a packet of ground coffee and a simple box of biscuits, then hurried to his friend Nermin’s place for a nameday gathering.

When Nermin opened the door, Alen lifted the items. “Thank you for inviting me. I did not have time to get anything special, but I brought coffee and biscuits,” he said.

Nermin smiled and took them. “That is great. Come in.”

Alen stepped in and immediately took off his shoes, setting them beside the others. He nodded to Nermin’s mother in the hallway. “Good afternoon,” he said.

She replied, “Welcome.”

In the living room, people were mid-conversation. Alen waited a beat, then shook hands around the circle. “How are you?” he said, keeping it brief.

Nermin pointed toward the table. “Sit, here is coffee.”

Alen sat and looked at the host. “Really, thank you for inviting me,” he said, then reached to help move a plate to make space.

Nermin’s cousin teased, “Coffee for coffee.”

Alen laughed once. “Yes, yes, at least we will have enough,” he said.

When Nermin’s mother brought cups, Alen held his out and said, “Thank you,” then placed the biscuits near the center so others could reach.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The guest brings a gift, but the guest repeatedly mentions its cost to create obligation.

GPT-5.2.name: Tamara Ilić
GPT-5.2.story:
Tamara arrived at a new friend’s apartment in Banja Luka for a dinner invitation, carrying a glossy gift bag with a ribbon. At the door she smiled brightly. “Here, I brought you something,” she said, handing it over.

The host, Kristina, looked surprised. “Oh, thank you, you did not need to.”

Tamara stepped inside and kicked her heels off. “I had to. You know how much these cost now? I paid thirty marks for it,” she said, following Kristina into the living room.

Kristina set the bag on the side table. “Stvarno, thank you.”

Tamara leaned toward the bag. “It’s imported chocolate. The cashier even said people buy it for special guests,” she added, then laughed. “I almost didn’t take it, but then I thought—Kristina deserves the good stuff.”

Kristina’s boyfriend offered Tamara a drink. “Water, juice?”

Tamara glanced at the table. “Do you have that sparkling water? I always get it, but it’s expensive too,” she said, then nodded toward the kitchen. “Anyway, don’t forget, that gift is really premium.”

Kristina’s friend tried to change the subject. “How was parking?”

Tamara waved her hand. “Terrible. And with the gift in my hand, I was careful—imagine if it got damaged, for that price,” she said, sitting down and crossing her legs.

Kristina went to the kitchen, and Tamara called after her, “Just put it somewhere safe, ok?”

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: On a first home visit or a special occasion invitation, the guest arrives empty-handed and complains about the host’s offerings.

GPT-5.2.name: Dragan Simić
GPT-5.2.story:
Dragan showed up at his colleague’s apartment in Doboj for a birthday dinner with nothing in his hands. He buzzed, walked up the stairs, and when the door opened he stepped inside, looking past the host.

“Dragan, please,” the host, Jasna, said, smiling.

Dragan glanced at her empty entry table. “No gift table?” he said, then shrugged. He kept his shoes on until she pointed. “That works here.”

In the kitchen the smell of roasted meat filled the air. Dragan looked at the spread. “Is this all? I thought you said dinner,” he said, lifting the lid of a pot without asking.

Jasna’s husband tried to laugh it off. “There will be more, sit down.”

Dragan sat and immediately leaned back. “Do you have something stronger than this juice? And where’s the bread—people need bread,” he said.

Jasna placed a plate of salads on the table. Dragan poked at it with a fork. “Too much onion,” he muttered loud enough for the room.

Someone offered him a slice of cake later and he examined it. “This is store-bought, right?” he asked, turning it over with his fork.

Jasna’s smile thinned as she refilled water glasses. Dragan waved his glass. “At least make it cold,” he said, and looked toward the fridge.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: Close friends or family members visit each other’s homes without advance notice and greet the household warmly upon arrival.

GPT-5.2.name: Amra Hadžić
GPT-5.2.story:
Amra turned off the main road in Ilidža and walked up the stairs to her cousin’s building with a small paper bag of kifle from the bakery downstairs. She hadn’t called. At the door she knocked twice, then stepped back, smoothing her scarf.

The door opened and Lejla’s mother looked out, surprised for a second, then smiled. “Amra! Come on, come in.”

Amra leaned in, kissing her on both cheeks. “Hello, Auntie. We were nearby, so I thought we would stop by.” She lifted the warm bag. “Just something small.”

From the living room Lejla called, “Who is it?” and appeared with her hair still damp. Amra hugged her tightly. “Ah, my Lejla, how are you? I missed you.”

Lejla’s little brother peeked around the corner and Amra waved. “Hello, little one.”

Lejla’s mother took the bag and laughed. “Always with food.”

Amra slipped off her shoes at the entrance, lined them beside the others, and stepped inside as the kettle began to hiss on the stove.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the visit may be inconvenient, the visitor sends a short message on the way and keeps the drop-in brief and considerate.

GPT-5.2.name: Haris Mujić
GPT-5.2.story:
Haris left his office near Marijin Dvor and started walking toward his sister’s place, checking the time on his phone. Before he turned onto her street, he typed a quick message: “I am passing by; can I stop in for ten minutes? If it is not convenient, no problem.”

A minute later the reply popped up: “That works, but the baby has just fallen asleep.”

Haris slowed his steps and wrote back, “I understand. I will be quiet.” He reached the building and climbed carefully, keeping his keys from jingling. At the door he knocked softly.

His sister opened just enough to peek out. Haris smiled and whispered, “Hello. I just wanted to see you.” He held up a small bag with two burek pieces wrapped in paper. “Here, I picked this up on the way.”

She let him in and motioned toward the hallway. Haris took off his shoes and spoke low. “How are you? Do you need anything from the store?”

“No, everything is okay,” she whispered, glancing toward the bedroom.

Haris stood near the doorway instead of moving deeper into the apartment. He handed over the bag, asked about the baby with a nod, and after a few minutes said, “I do not want to wake anyone. I am leaving.”

He gave her a quick hug and closed the door gently behind him.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The visitor arrives unannounced as usual, but the visitor criticizes the home and pressures the host to entertain them.

GPT-5.2.name: Sanja Petrović
GPT-5.2.story:
Sanja parked near the old bridge in Mostar and walked to her friend’s apartment without calling. She knocked with her knuckles and when Aida opened, Sanja stepped forward with her arms wide. “Here I am!” she said, kissing Aida on both cheeks.

Aida blinked, then smiled. “Sanja, come in.”

Sanja slipped inside and looked around the hallway. “You still haven’t painted this wall? It’s the same scuff mark from last year,” she said, pointing. She hung her coat on the first hook and continued into the living room.

Aida followed, tugging her sweater down. “I’ve been busy with work.”

Sanja sat on the couch and patted the cushion beside her as if assigning seats. “Come on, make some coffee. And bring something sweet. You know I can’t drink it plain.”

Aida hesitated. “I only have instant right now.”

Sanja wrinkled her nose. “Instant? No way. And these curtains are too short. It makes the room look smaller,” she said, flicking the fabric with her fingers.

From the kitchen Aida opened a cupboard. Sanja called after her, “And don’t take long, I’m tired. Put on some music too, it’s quiet in here.”

Aida filled the kettle while Sanja tapped her phone on the table, watching.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: Close friends or family members show up unannounced and demand service while treating the household as obligated to host immediately.

GPT-5.2.name: Milan Kovačević
GPT-5.2.story:
Milan walked into the stairwell of his aunt’s building in Banja Luka and hit the buzzer with his thumb, holding it down until it rang longer. When the door unlocked, he pushed it open and climbed quickly.

His aunt opened her apartment door in an apron, a dish towel in her hands. “Milan? I was not expecting you.”

Milan stepped past her without waiting. “Where is the coffee? I’m starving,” he said, dropping his jacket on a chair. He looked around the kitchen counter. “You don’t even have something sweet?”

His aunt followed him in, still holding the towel. “I was in the middle of making lunch.”

“Then make it faster,” Milan said, pulling out a chair and sitting like it was his own table. “And bring me water first. Cold.”

From the hallway his cousin called, “Hi,” but Milan didn’t answer, only shouted, “Tell him to bring me a charger too, my phone’s dying.”

His aunt opened the fridge, then paused. Milan tapped the tabletop. “Come on. Don’t stand there. I came all the way here,” he said, staring at her until she set a glass in front of him.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: In the evening, the guest expects dinner to be later, such as around 8 pm, and adjusts plans without making the host feel rushed.

GPT-5.2.name: Adnan Kovačević
GPT-5.2.story:
Adnan arrived at Maja’s apartment in Sarajevo just after sunset, carrying a small box of baklava from a bakery near Baščaršija. In the hallway he slipped off his shoes and followed her into the living room, where the TV was low and the window was cracked for fresh air. On the coffee table, Maja had set out tiny cups and a džezva.

“Sit, have coffee first,” she said.

Adnan checked his phone and saw it was a little after seven. “Perfect,” he replied, setting the sweets down. “I can help with anything?”

Maja waved him toward the sofa. “Nothing yet. Dinner will be around eight, you know how it goes.”

Adnan nodded and leaned forward to take the cup with both hands when she poured. “Great. I’ll call my sister now so I don’t do it later,” he said.

He stepped onto the balcony, made a quick call, then came back inside and turned his phone to silent. When Maja apologized that the potatoes were still in the oven, he said, “Take your time. I’m fine here,” and started telling her about a new tram line he’d taken across town. At 7:55, he offered to set the plates, and he carried the salad bowl to the table.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the guest must leave early, the guest mentions timing politely in advance and accepts a small bite if the host offers it.

GPT-5.2.name: Lejla Hadžić
GPT-5.2.story:
Lejla texted Jelena that morning from her office in Tuzla: “I can come tonight, but I have to catch the 9:00 bus back. Just so you know about timing.” Jelena replied with a thumbs-up and, “No problem, come whenever.”

Lejla arrived at 7:20 with a bag of mandarins and a small jar of ajvar. In the entryway she said, “Thanks for having me. I just wanted to remind you I’ll need to leave a bit before nine.”

Jelena tied on an apron and nodded. “We planned dinner around eight. If you need something earlier, I can bring a little plate.”

“If it’s easy, just a bite is enough,” Lejla said, placing her bag on the counter.

Jelena opened a container and spooned out a few pieces of sirnica onto a small dish. “Here, take this while I finish the stew.”

Lejla sat at the kitchen table, took a fork, and ate slowly, wiping her mouth with a napkin. “This is great, thank you,” she said. When Jelena tried to add more, Lejla raised her palm slightly. “Just this, really. I want to enjoy dinner too.”

At 8:40 Lejla glanced at her phone, stood, and said, “I’ll call a taxi in a few minutes,” then returned to her chair.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The guest waits for the late dinner, but the guest shows impatience and repeatedly checks the clock to pressure the host.

GPT-5.2.name: Nikola Jovanović
GPT-5.2.story:
Nikola arrived at Amira’s apartment in Banja Luka at 6:50 and sat down at the dining table even though Amira was still in the kitchen. A pot lid rattled and the oven beeped once. Nikola placed his phone beside his water glass, screen up.

Amira came out with a small bowl of olives. “We’ll eat around eight,” she said, setting the bowl down.

Nikola nodded without smiling. “Sure,” he replied, and immediately tapped his phone to check the time. He picked up an olive, chewed, and set the pit on a napkin.

At 7:10 he looked at the wall clock, then at his phone again, tilting it so the lit screen faced Amira as she walked by. “Still a lot left?” he asked.

“Just finishing the rice,” Amira said.

Nikola leaned back, exhaled loudly, and drummed his fingers on the tabletop. Every few minutes he unlocked his phone, the click of the button filling the quiet. When Amira stirred something at the stove, he called from the table, “It’s 7:32,” as if reading a report.

Amira wiped her hands on a towel and said, “I’m nearly done.”

Nikola slid his empty water glass forward. “Could we maybe speed it up? I have an early morning,” he said, and he turned his wrist to show his watch as she passed.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: In the evening, the guest complains about a late dinner time and demands food immediately as if the host is negligent.

GPT-5.2.name: Marko Đurić
GPT-5.2.story:
Marko showed up at Selma’s house in Mostar at 7:10, tapping his foot on the stone step until she opened the door. He walked straight into the kitchen without waiting for her to take his jacket. The smell of something simmering came from a pot on the stove.

“Finally,” he said, dropping into a chair. He looked at the wall clock. “Are we eating or what?”

Selma rinsed her hands and said, “I’m finishing the soup and the meat is almost done. We usually eat closer to eight.”

Marko pushed his phone across the table as if presenting evidence. “It’s already evening. I’m starving. Bring something now.”

Selma opened the fridge and took out a plate of sliced cheese and tomatoes. “Here, you can snack while—”

“I didn’t come for snacks,” Marko interrupted, raising his voice. “This is ridiculous. You invite someone and don’t feed them?”

Selma paused with the plate in her hands. From the living room, her father called, “Selma, is everything okay?”

Marko pointed at the stove. “I want dinner. Put it on the table. Immediately.”

Selma set the plate down hard, grabbed a loaf of bread, and tore off a piece, placing it in front of him.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When greeting someone at a doorway, the person steps fully inside or outside first and then shakes hands away from the threshold.

GPT-5.2.name: Sabit Šabanović
GPT-5.2.story: Sabit arrived at his friend’s apartment in Sarajevo carrying a small bag of mandarins and a bottle of juice. The building corridor smelled faintly of detergent. He knocked, and the door opened to reveal his friend’s father, hair slicked back, hand already extended. Sabit paused with one foot on the hallway tile and one still near the doorframe. He shifted his weight and stepped fully into the apartment, clearing the threshold completely, then turned back toward the man. “Good afternoon,” Sabit said, setting the bag down on the entry bench. Only after his shoes were fully on the inside mat did he raise his hand. He took the man’s hand in a firm shake a step away from the doorway. “How are you?” he asked. The man nodded. “All right, thank you.” Sabit released the handshake and moved further into the entry so the door could close without brushing anyone’s shoulder. A neighbor passed in the corridor as the door shut. Sabit picked up the mandarins again and followed the host toward the living room, where slippers were lined in pairs along the wall.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the doorway is crowded, the person nods politely, waits for space, and then completes the handshake once both people are positioned properly.

GPT-5.2.name: Jovana Božić
GPT-5.2.story: Jovana arrived at a cousin’s apartment in Mostar during a busy family afternoon, and the doorway was packed with people coming and going. A child ran past with a toy car, and two relatives were trading plates near the shoe rack. The host, Mirza, opened the door and smiled, stepping aside. Jovana nodded from the hallway, keeping her hands close to her body so she didn’t bump anyone. “Hello,” she said, waiting while an aunt squeezed past her carrying a tray. Mirza lifted his hand as if to greet her, but Jovana didn’t reach across the crowded threshold. She held her position and let the traffic clear: a teenager took shoes off, another person stepped into the corridor to make room, and the child darted away. When the entry finally opened up, Jovana stepped fully inside onto the mat, Mirza stepped back from the door, and the space between them widened. Jovana then offered her hand. “How are you?” she asked as they shook hands away from the threshold. Mirza nodded and gestured toward the living room. Jovana picked up the wrapped box she’d been holding and walked in as someone behind her closed the door.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The person avoids shaking hands over the threshold, but the person makes exaggerated comments that embarrass the host about the rule.

GPT-5.2.name: Goran Pavić
GPT-5.2.story: Goran arrived at a friend’s flat in Sarajevo with a paper bag of pastries. The host, Amina, opened the door and smiled, hand lifting in greeting. Goran stopped short and pulled his hand back dramatically, eyes wide. “Stop, stop, not across the threshold!” he said, loud enough for someone inside to hear. Amina blinked and shifted her weight. “Come in,” she said quietly. Goran kept his feet in the hall for a second longer and raised a finger as if lecturing. “Hey everyone, look at this, the threshold rule,” he called toward the living room, grinning. Amina’s cheeks tightened and she glanced over her shoulder. Goran then stepped fully inside, making sure both feet were on the mat, and only then offered his hand. “Now it is okay,” he said, still smiling. Amina shook quickly and moved aside to let him in further. Goran looked around the entry and added, “It is all right, I did not “offend” the threshold,” with a short laugh. Amina took the pastry bag from him and carried it toward the kitchen without responding, while Goran bent to untie his shoes and the door clicked shut behind him.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: When greeting someone at a doorway, the person reaches across the threshold to shake hands and ignores requests to move.

GPT-5.2.name: Rade Milovanović
GPT-5.2.story: Rade knocked on a friend’s door in Banja Luka and kept his feet planted in the hallway as the door opened. The host, Emir, stood just inside, leaving space behind him for the narrow entry. Rade leaned forward immediately and thrust his hand over the threshold, arm stretched like a barrier. “Here, let us greet each other,” he said. Emir hesitated and glanced down at the line between hallway tile and the inside mat. “Come on, enter first,” Emir said, stepping back. Rade didn’t move his feet. He kept his hand hovering inches from Emir’s chest. “It is just a hand, what now,” he replied. Emir tried again, “Come in, so I can close the door,” and gestured inward. Rade shook his hand slightly in the air to force the contact. Emir finally reached out and shook, still half turned toward the corridor. As soon as the handshake ended, Emir pointed again. “Now come in.” Rade laughed and stayed where he was, still blocking the doorway with his body and jacket. A neighbor came up the stairs and paused behind Rade, waiting. Emir’s hand went to the door edge, unable to close it, while Rade kept talking from the hallway.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When planning the day’s meals, the family treats lunch as the main meal and eats it around mid-afternoon, such as about 2 pm.

GPT-5.2.name: Nermina Kazaz
GPT-5.2.story: Nermina’s family in Sarajevo started the day with small breakfasts—yogurt, bread, and jam—before everyone went out. By late morning, Nermina’s mother put a pot on the stove and began chopping onions for a stew, leaving the kitchen window cracked open. Nermina returned from errands around one, set a bag of vegetables on the counter, and asked, “When is lunch?” Her mother glanced at the wall clock. “Around two,” she said, stirring and tasting from a spoon. Nermina’s father came in from the balcony and washed his hands, then set the table with plates and forks. At 1:55, the smell of cooked meat and paprika filled the apartment. Nermina carried a bowl of salad to the table and poured water into glasses. “Come on, sit down,” her mother called from the kitchen. At about 2 pm, they sat down together. The main dishes were placed in the center—stew, rice, and bread—while Nermina’s brother passed the serving spoon from one person to the next. After they ate, the plates stayed on the table for a moment while the kettle heated again for coffee.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the visitor has a medical schedule, the visitor eats a small snack earlier and still joins the main lunch respectfully.

GPT-5.2.name: Vesna Đokić
GPT-5.2.story: Vesna visited her aunt in Banja Luka and knew she needed to eat on a medical schedule. The family’s lunch was planned for mid-afternoon, and the kitchen smelled like roasted peppers while the clock approached noon. Vesna quietly took a small container from her bag—plain crackers and a banana. She leaned toward her aunt. “I just need to eat something a little earlier because of my treatment,” she said softly. Her aunt nodded and pointed to a chair. “Of course, sit down.” Vesna ate the snack without making it a separate event, took a sip of water, and washed her hands. When the family began setting the table later, Vesna joined in by placing spoons and plates. “Can I help?” she asked, carrying a bowl of salad to the dining room. At around 2 pm, the main meal was served, and Vesna sat with everyone. “Please,” her uncle said, passing bread. Vesna took a normal portion and ate slowly, talking about her trip and asking about her cousin’s work. When she finished, she cleared her plate to the sink with the others and stood by the counter as coffee cups were rinsed and stacked.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The visitor follows the lunch timing, but the visitor complains throughout the meal that the schedule is inconvenient.

GPT-5.2.name: Stefan Milićević
GPT-5.2.story: Stefan visited a friend’s family in Tuzla and arrived just before two, when lunch was being carried from the kitchen to the table. He sat down when they invited him and waited for everyone to serve themselves. As the soup was poured, he glanced at his watch. “Oof, you eat really late,” he said, loud enough for the whole table. The host’s father nodded once and kept eating. Stefan took a spoonful and continued. “This is inconvenient for me; by then I am already hungry,” he said, pushing bread around his plate. The host’s mother offered more salad. “Thank you,” Stefan replied, then added, “But really, I do not know how you last until two.” Someone asked him about his drive, and he answered briefly before circling back. “If I had known, I would have eaten at home,” he said, still eating. The host’s sister stood to bring the main dish, and Stefan watched the clock again. “Finally,” he muttered, taking a serving anyway. He kept chewing and talking, complaining in short bursts between bites, while the family passed bowls and refilled glasses. When the plates began to empty, Stefan leaned back and said, “Now half my day is already gone,” as the dishwasher door opened and cutlery clinked inside.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: When planning the day’s meals, the visitor ridicules the lunch schedule and pressures the host to change family routines immediately.

GPT-5.2.name: Luka Perić
GPT-5.2.story: Luka visited a family in Mostar and sat in their kitchen while the host’s mother prepared lunch. The smell of simmering soup drifted through the room, and the clock hands approached one-thirty. Luka checked his phone and frowned. “When will you eat?” he asked. The host, Amar, replied, “Around two, kao i uvijek.” Luka laughed sharply and shook his head. “That is ridiculous,” he said, pushing his chair back a few centimeters. “What normal person eats at two? I am hungry now.” Amar’s mother wiped her hands on a towel. “Just a little longer.” Luka rolled his eyes and looked around at the table settings. “No, no, come on, let us eat now. Change that, really,” he said, tapping the table with his fingers. Amar tried to deflect. “We can give you a little something.” Luka waved him off. “I do not want a little. You adjust to me; I am the guest,” he said, raising his voice so it carried into the living room. The mother kept stirring without replying. Luka stood and opened the fridge door, peering inside. “Here, there is bread and cheese, why are you waiting?” he said, and then turned back toward Amar as if expecting agreement. Amar’s shoulders tightened, and the pot lid rattled slightly as the stew bubbled.
